title: Just A Thought: A Matter of Style
author: Vern Seward, The Mac Observer (quoting Reuters / Peter Kastner)
abstract: Mac Observer 'Just A Thought' column by Vern Seward (Apr 16 2004) arguing that Apple's decades-long emphasis on design and style has finally prompted PC makers to rethink bulky tower form factors for the living room. The column embeds a Reuters 'Small Is Huge in PCs These Days' article with Peter Kastner's prescient observation: 'The next form factor battles are beginning to be fought in the living room.' Seward is skeptical — he believes people won't want a PC in the living room competing with the TV, though he grants that content-creation-capable home PCs do have a role. The column is a snapshot of the 2004 moment when PC OEMs (HP, Sony, Dell) began investing in living-room-friendly designs (Media Center PCs, iPod-mini style envy).
importance_rationale: Mac-community opinion column; embeds a Kastner Reuters quote that is the more-cited artifact. Not a primary analyst report, but preserves Kastner's living-room form-factor framing as it propagated into Apple-focused press.
relevance_rationale: Living-room-PC thesis evolved into home-theater PCs, then streaming boxes (Apple TV, Roku, Chromecast). The form-factor-matters argument underpins Apple's current Mac mini / HomePod lineup and the broader ambient-computing trend.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's 'next form factor battles fought in the living room' was partially correct: Windows Media Center PCs (2004-2015) and the Apple TV / streaming-box category (2007+) both contested the living room, though most consumers never adopted a general-purpose PC there. Seward's skepticism about full PCs in the living room was also validated — streaming appliances won, not PCs.
